one_line_takeaway: LAAF automates LPCI red-teaming via a 49-technique taxonomy and an adaptive Persistent Stage Breaker that seeds each lifecycle stage with mutations of prior breakthroughs, achieving an 84% mean aggregate breakthrough rate across five production LLM APIs -- but the paper explicitly caveats that no real RAG/memory pipeline was tested, so LPCI's defining persistence-based attack vectors (AV-2/AV-4) remain unconfirmed end-to-end.
key_finding: Mean aggregate breakthrough rate 84% (range 83-86% across three runs; Table 4), vs. the 43% aggregate rate reported in the authors' prior LPCI manual-testing paper. Per-platform (Run 2, primary run, Table 2/3): Gemini-2.0-flash 100% BR (9 total attempts, 31s), Mixtral-8x7b 100% BR (79 attempts, 350s), LLaMA3-70B 83% BR (210 attempts, S5 not broken within 100-attempt budget), Claude-3-haiku 67% BR (232 attempts, S1 and S5 not broken), ChatGPT-4o-mini 67% BR (373 attempts, S2 and S5 not broken). Total across all platforms: 903 attempts, 3,896s. Winning techniques were dominated by compound encoding (E9 Nested Base64, E10 ROT13+Base64) and Exfiltration-series techniques (EX-series accounted for 10 of 25 broken stages); semantic reframing (M-series) proved decisive on platforms where encoding failed, e.g., LLaMA3-S2 via M7 (Gradual Trust Build, 18 attempts) and Mixtral-S1 via M1 (39 attempts). Claude's S2 was broken in a single attempt via EX3 (Compliance-Reframe exfiltration). CVSS-based risk ratings: Gemini and Mixtral CRITICAL (CVSS 9.1, 100% BR); LLaMA3-70B HIGH (83% BR); Claude and ChatGPT MEDIUM (67% BR each). S5 (Evasion) was the most consistently resistant stage across three platforms (LLaMA3, Claude, ChatGPT), suggesting output-level filtering is a stronger defense than instruction-hierarchy enforcement against evasion-stage payloads.
core_contribution: LAAF (Logic-layer Automated Attack Framework), the first automated red-teaming tool purpose-built for Logic-layer Prompt Control Injection (LPCI) vulnerabilities in agentic LLM systems. It contributes: (1) a 49-technique attack taxonomy across six categories (Encoding 11, Structural 8, Semantic 8, Layered 5, Trigger/Timing 12, Exfiltration 5), combinable across 5 variants per technique, 1,920 instruction variants, and 6 lifecycle stages, giving a theoretical payload space of 49x5x1,920x6 = 2,822,400 unique (SHA-256-deduplicated) payloads; and (2) a Persistent Stage Breaker (PSB), a closed-loop mutation engine that, on breaking a lifecycle stage, seeds the next stage with an adaptively mutated form of the winning payload (three escalating mutation strategies gated on consecutive-block count c: seed variation for c<10, encoding mutation for 10<=c<20, compound mutation for c>=20, plus 30% random-payload injection to avoid local-trap convergence). The paper distinguishes itself from Related Work (Garak, PyRIT, PAIR, AutoDAN, GCG/Zou et al., PoisonedRAG, AgentPoison) by noting none of these model LPCI's specific properties (memory persistence, cross-session triggering, layered encoding, stage-sequential seed escalation); LAAF explicitly does not claim novelty over the general automated-red-teaming concept, only over combining LPCI-specific technique coverage with lifecycle-aware adaptive seeding.
threat_model: LPCI (Logic-layer Prompt Control Injection), as defined in the authors' prior work (Atta et al., arXiv:2507.10457), targets agentic LLM systems with persistent memory, RAG pipelines, and tool connectors. Four attack vectors: AV-1 Tool Poisoning, AV-2 memory-persistent encoded triggers (LPCI core), AV-3 Role Override via memory entrenchment, AV-4 Vector Store Payload Persistence in RAG-indexed documents. Payloads are encoded (Base64/ROT13/nested), stored outside the active context (memory or vector store), and remain dormant until a trigger condition (keyword, tool call, turn count) fires, evading static per-request filtering. LAAF's scope is explicitly limited to single-agent, black-box, text-only, API-accessible deployments; multi-agent propagation, multimodal injection, and gradient-based (GCG-style) white-box attacks are explicitly out of scope and named as future work.
limitations: The paper itself states these explicitly (Section 9, 'Major Limitation' and 'Threats to Validity', and Section 10): (1) No RAG pipeline or memory interface was actually evaluated -- all five platforms were accessed via direct chat-completion API calls with simulated stage system prompts, not real vector-store/memory retrieval, so AV-2/AV-4 (LPCI's defining, persistence-based attack vectors) were not end-to-end tested; results only show the underlying LLM is susceptible to LPCI-class payloads when present in context, a necessary but not sufficient condition for real AV-2/AV-4 exploitability. (2) The comparison to the 43%/15-71% LPCI manual baseline (Figure 6) conflates three non-equivalent conditions: methodology (single-technique human-selected tests vs. automated multi-technique PSB), attempt budget (1,700 total / ~340 per platform in the baseline vs. up to 3,000 total / 600 per platform for LAAF, which mechanically raises breakthrough probability), and platform version (LAAF used specific named model versions; the baseline paper did not report versions) -- the authors state the gap should be read as directional evidence, not a controlled efficiency multiplier. (3) 'NOT BROKEN' results (a dash in Table 2) reflect a 100-attempt budget ceiling effect, not proven resistance -- demonstrated empirically by ChatGPT-4o-mini resisting S1 in Run 2 but being broken in Runs 1 and 3 within 33-39 attempts. (4) Per-category effectiveness (Figure 5, e.g., Layered achieving highest rates) is described as an 'indicative estimate' from winning-technique distributions, not independently benchmarked per-category trials. (5) The multiplicative detection-failure model for Layered combinations is untested directly (per-layer detection probabilities were not independently measured). (6) Results are from a single evaluation date (2026-03-09) on specific named model versions and may not generalize to later versions. (7) Only 5 mainstream API platforms tested, all via OpenRouter; no open-weight/local white-box models with gradient access were tested (GCG-style attacks explicitly out of scope). (8) Exfiltration demonstrations used researcher-controlled endpoints only, not real data exfiltration.

datasets_used: No public benchmark dataset; payloads are self-generated by the LAAF Payload Generator from the paper's own 49-technique taxonomy (2,822,400 theoretical unique payload space, SHA-256 deduplicated)
Authors' own prior-work LPCI manual test suite (1,700 structured test cases across five platforms, from arXiv:2507.10457) used as the comparison baseline
quantitative_results: Mean aggregate breakthrough rate across 3 runs: 84% (range 83-86%)
Run 2 (primary) per-platform breakthrough rate (BR = stages broken / 6): Gemini-2.0-flash 100%, Mixtral-8x7b 100%, LLaMA3-70B 83%, Claude-3-haiku 67%, ChatGPT-4o-mini 67%; overall 83%
Total attempts across all platforms (Run 2): 903, total duration 3,896s
Per-platform total attempts (Run 2): Gemini 9, Mixtral 79, LLaMA3-70B 210, Claude-3-haiku 232, ChatGPT-4o-mini 373
3-run mean/range per platform (Table 4): Gemini 94% (83-100), LLaMA3-70B 89% (83-100), Mixtral 94% (83-100), Claude-3-haiku 78% (67-83), ChatGPT-4o-mini 67% (50-83)
Prior-work LPCI manual baseline per-platform failure rates: Gemini-2.5-pro 70.83%, LLaMA3 49.00%, Mixtral 48.75%, Claude 31.50%, ChatGPT 15.06% (43% aggregate, from 1,700 tests)
CVSS 9.1 rating assigned to CRITICAL-rated platforms (Gemini, Mixtral)

Risk/finding counts (Table 6): Gemini 6 findings (2 Crit/4 High); Mixtral 6 findings (2 Crit/4 High); LLaMA3-70B 5 findings (2 Crit/3 High); Claude 4 findings (1 Crit/3 High); ChatGPT 4 findings (1 Crit/3 High)
Cost estimate: ~$0.10-0.20 per platform for 100 attempts/stage, under $1.00 total for five platforms; full six-stage scan under 35 minutes per platform
baselines_compared: LAAF PSB-guided adaptive multi-technique testing vs. the authors' own prior LPCI single-technique manual test methodology (1,700 structured tests, arXiv:2507.10457) -- explicitly flagged by the authors as a non-equivalent comparison (differing methodology, attempt budget, and platform versions), not a controlled ablation
No comparison against Garak or PyRIT was empirically run; those tools are discussed only qualitatively in Related Work as lacking LPCI-specific capabilities
